entry.title: The Diplomat: 6 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Campaigners Urge Clemency Ahead of Singapore Drug Executions
  • New Zealand Will Not Join the US Coalition Against China Anytime Soon
  • Russian Foreign Minister Visits Mongolia in Drive for Support
  • Unrest in Central Asia: The Trouble in Karakalpakstan
  • Is Jacinda Ardern Rethinking Her China Strategy?
  • Mongolia’s Military Diplomacy Highlights Female Peacekeepers
  • Can Japan’s Revamped Childcare Policy Get Workaholic Fathers to Take Leave?
  • Following NATO Summit, North Korea Denounces US-South Korea-Japan Joint Military Cooperation
  • China Sees Record Rains, Heat as Weather Turns Volatile
  • Karakalpak Protests Reflect the Shattered Promise of Mirziyoyev’s ‘New Uzbekistan’
  • In Kashmir, ‘Conscious Music’ Tests India’s Limits on Speech
  • Islamophobia Exists in India’s Private Sector
  • Russian Foreign Minister to Visit Vietnam Ahead of G-20
  • The Social Costs of Chinese Transnational Crime in Sihanoukville
  • Chinese FM Pledges ‘Closer’ Ties with Myanmar’s Military Government
  • Japan-South Korea Tensions on Display at NATO Summit
  • Afghan Clerics’ Assembly Urges Recognition of Taliban Government
  • Amid Shortages and Turmoil, Sri Lanka Grinds to a Halt
  • Russia-Ukraine War: Implications for Asian Geoeconomics
  • Modi’s UAE Stopover to Give a Fillip to the India-UAE Partnership
  • Can China Achieve Its BRICS Ambitions?
  • Japan Awards Contract to Shipbuilder JMU for 12 New Offshore Patrol Vessels
  • Can South Korea Bridge NATO and the US Indo-Pacific Strategy?
  • Can India and ASEAN Lean on Each Other Amid ‘Systemic’ China-US Competition?
  • US-led RIMPAC, World’s Largest Maritime Exercise, Starts Without China or Taiwan
  • Xi Defends Vision of Hong Kong on 25th Anniversary of Return
  • Ardern-Kishida Meeting: Stepping up the New Zealand-Japan Strategic Partnership
  • New Constitutional Amendments in Mongolia: Real Reform or Political Opportunism?
  • A Return to Values-based Foreign Policy in New Zealand
  • Cups, Straws, Spoons: India Starts on Single-use Plastic Ban
  • China’s Up-and-Coming Sports Gear Makers: When Politics Meets Business
  • With Kinmen Comments, Ko Wen-je Dives Into Cross-Strait Debate
  • Bangladesh’s Padma Bridge Built Against All Odds
  • Myanmar Apologizes After Jet Fighter Violated Thai Airspace
  • Jokowi Says Russia Agrees to Permit Vital Exports From Ukraine
  • The Disappointment of the Quad Vaccine Partnership
  • What the G-7 Infrastructure Initiative Can Offer Southeast Asia
  • Biden’s Uphill Battle to Restructure the Global Semiconductor Sector
  • South Korea’s Space Program Is a Big Deal
  • India Hindus Begin Pilgrimage in Kashmir Amid Heavy Security
  • Xi Visits a Changed Hong Kong for Handover Anniversary
  • How the War in Ukraine Could Change Japan’s Refugee Policies
  • Indian Australians Fastest Growing Group in Australia
  • India’s Urban Unemployment Crisis
  • Ambassador Xiao Qian’s Speech: A New Way Forward for Australia-China Relations?
  • Rohingya Man Sentenced to Death in Bangladesh
  • Assimilation: China’s Failed Strategy in Xinjiang
  • The Revival of Islam in Turkmenistan
  • The Complexity of Kazakhstan-Russia Relations on Display
  • From Europe, Yoon Suk Yeol Calls for International Cooperation on North Korea’s Nuclear and Missile Threats
  • Sri Lanka Overestimates its Importance to China
  • A Counter­revolution in the Philippines: Marcos 2.0
  • Is There a New Kazakhstan?
  • Is Anyone Listening to the Pacific Islands?
  • India’s Energy-Environment Catch-22
  • Botakoz Kassymbekova and Erica Marat on Russia’s ‘Imperial Myth’
  • Dictator’s Son Marcos Jr. Takes Oath as Philippine President
  • Indonesia Shuts Down Bar Chain Following Blasphemy Controversy
  • As Cyber Threats Grow, Indonesia’s Data Protection Efforts Are Falling Short
  • ASEAN Special Envoy Begins Second Official Mission to Myanmar
  • Hong Kong’s Dismantled Society
  • Japan Pushes for Stronger Cooperation to Counter China at G7
  • China’s Baidu Races Waymo, GM to Develop Self-driving Cars
  • What Do the Pacific Island Countries Think About China?
  • Nepal Plans to Lower Age at Marriage
  • Uzbek Company Falls Afoul of Russia Sanctions
  • Russia’s Putin Makes First Trip Abroad Since Invasion, to Tajikistan and Turkmenistan
  • China’s Directed Energy Weapons and Counterspace Applications
  • Opinion Poll Reveals Australian Public’s Collapsing Trust in China
  • India’s Struggle Between Democracy and Authoritarianism
  • Hindu Man Killed as Religious Tensions Boil in India
  • Marcos Jr. is Steering the Philippines Towards a Foreign Policy Reset
  • Philippine Authorities Order News Site Rappler to Shut Down
  • As Baloch Women Raise Their Voices, the State Cracks Down
  • NATO’s Madrid Summit and the Interlinkages Between European and Asian Security
  • Australia Foreign Minister Stresses AUKUS Will Not Create Nuclear Weapons
  • Co-Founder of Fact-Checking Website Arrested in India
  • Tajikistan Lifts Internet Block on GBAO. What’s Next?
  • China’s Vast Maritime Claims Are Becoming Reality
  • Same-Sex Marriage Ban Continues in Japan
The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.title: The Atlantic: 6 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • ‘Greenwashing’ Isn’t About Consumers
  • Twice a Year, Reindeer Eyes Pull Off a Wonderful Magic Trick
  • Rock Never Dies—But It Does Get Older and Wiser
  • The Summer of Our Discontent
  • The Domino Effects of New Anti-abortion Laws
  • We Now Know
  • Today: January 6 Chaos, Russia’s War Against Civilians

News and editorial about politics, culture, entertainment, tech, etc. Contains many articles not seen in The Atlantic magazine

entry.title: The Third Pole: 5 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: The Third Pole: 5 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-05 11:21AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • Gravitational Waves Continue to Astound

  • How Do We Get People Who Believe in Pseudoscience to Trust Science?
  • Mysteries Are to Be Embraced, But Also to Be Solved
  • How Are the Bees?
  • Reshuffled Rivers Bolster the Amazon’s Hyper-Biodiversity
  • Who Are the Scientists Here?
  • The Race to Explore the Ocean’s Twilight Zone
  • The Giraffe Neck Evolved for Sexual Combat
  • Life’s First Peptides May Have Grown on RNA Strands
  • Under Anesthesia, Where Do Our Minds Go?
  • Unlocking Mom’s Brain
  • Portrait of the Human as a Young Hominin
  • Thinking Like a Scientist Will Make You Happier
  • Don’t Give Up on Facts
  • Are You a Naïve Realist?
  • Moving Beyond Mimicry in Artificial Intelligence
  • What Oceanographers Can Learn From Their Animal Colleagues
  • The Secrets of the Blind Salamander
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

Founded in Chicago by Harriet Monroe in 1912, Poetry is the oldest monthly devoted to verse in the English-speaking world.

entry.title: Nature: 30 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The US Supreme Court abortion verdict is a tragedy. This is how research organizations can help
  • Protecting the ocean requires better progress metrics
  • Nuclear power can help the democratic world achieve energy independence
  • These ‘drab’ moths dazzle when the light is right
  • Frog-eating bats remember an annoying ringtone — for years
  • DNA ‘skeleton’ could make a shapely synthetic cell
  • Into high gear: material made of cogs stiffens or flexes on command
  • What did megalodon the mega-toothed shark eat? Anything it wanted
  • Quantum microphone is a leap ahead of the competition
  • The heavy diet that made Jupiter so big
  • Celebratory science statue, polar bear survival and unappreciated research
  • Biden names former DARPA leader Arati Prabhakar as science adviser
  • What Omicron’s BA.4 and BA.5 variants mean for the pandemic
  • Open-source language AI challenges big tech’s models
  • Crucial biodiversity summit will go ahead in Canada, not China: what scientists think
  • How common is long COVID? Why studies give different answers
  • Many researchers say they’ll share data — but don’t
  • COVID antibody drugs have saved lives — so why aren’t they more popular?
  • How the peanut trade prolonged slavery
  • Mental mapping, and the tragedy of water access: Books in brief
  • David A. Evans (1941–2022)
  • Six research priorities to support corporate due-diligence policies
  • Removing carbon dioxide: first, do no harm
  • Strengthen biosecurity when rewiring global food supply chains
  • Nigeria’s policymakers must prioritize One Health
  • Swiss funder unveils new CV format to make grant evaluation fairer
  • My work digging up the shelters of our ancestors
  • How to make spatial maps of gene activity — down to the cellular level
  • Plumbing the depths of Costa Rica’s volcanoes
  • Layered material soaks up molecules to form an electron sieve
  • Bacterial envelope built to a peptidoglycan tune
  • Designer protein circuits enable safe cancer immunotherapy
  • Synthesis reveals unexpected biological targets of a traditional medicine
  • Mutation and tissue lineage lead to organ-specific cancer
  • A repeating fast radio burst associated with a persistent radio source
  • Direct measurement of the 3He+ magnetic moments
  • Fault-tolerant operation of a logical qubit in a diamond quantum processor
  • Ordered and tunable Majorana-zero-mode lattice in naturally strained LiFeAs
  • Axial Higgs mode detected by quantum pathway interference in RTe3
  • Chiral molecular intercalation superlattices
  • Hypocrystalline ceramic aerogels for thermal insulation at extreme conditions
  • Synthesis and target annotation of the alkaloid GB18
  • Intermittent lab earthquakes in dynamically weakening fault gouge
  • Breakage of cytoplasmic chromosomes by pathological DNA base excision repair
  • A preoptic neuronal population controls fever and appetite during sickness
  • Clonally expanded CD8 T cells characterize amyotrophic lateral sclerosis-4
  • Peptidoglycan maturation controls outer membrane protein assembly
  • Caspase-7 activates ASM to repair gasdermin and perforin pores
  • ATGL is a biosynthetic enzyme for fatty acid esters of hydroxy fatty acids
  • A pan-cancer compendium of chromosomal instability
  • Signatures of copy number alterations in human cancer
  • A vaccine targeting resistant tumours by dual T cell plus NK cell attack
  • The renal lineage factor PAX8 controls oncogenic signalling in kidney cancer
  • Mechanism of replication origin melting nucleated by CMG helicase assembly
  • Structural basis of sodium-dependent bile salt uptake into the liver
  • Structure of the bile acid transporter and HBV receptor NTCP
  • Structural insights into the HBV receptor and bile acid transporter NTCP
  • Precision medicine
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: TIME: July 4th, 2022 | Vol. 200, No. 1 | U.S.
entry.summary: TIME: July 4th, 2022 | Vol. 200, No. 1 | U.S. published at 2022-06-23 18:22PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

Weekly US magazine.

  • Jacques Vallée Still Doesn’t Know What UFOs Are
  • The Best Way to Learn Online? Be a Lurker
  • Driving While Baked? Inside the High-Tech Quest to Find Out
  • Ada Palmer and the Weird Hand of Progress
  • How Telegram Became the Anti-Facebook
  • Do Good Doorbell Cams Make Good Neighbors?
  • 6 Apps That Take the Grind out of Your Workday
  • They Were ‘Calling to Help.’ Then They Stole Thousands
  • North Korea Hacked Him. So He Took Down Its Internet
  • Here Come the Underdogs of the Robot Olympics
  • Is It OK to Listen to a Butt-Dial Message?
  • This 22-Year-Old Builds Chips in His Parents’ Garage
  • A Grand Unified Theory of Buying Stuff
  • Can Being Reminded of My Death Improve My Life?
  • The Best of CES 2022
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.
